context:
People in this club who perform in school talent shows often attend and are very engaged with school events.
People in this club either perform in school talent shows often or are inactive and disinterested community members.
People in this club who chaperone high school dances are not students who attend the school.
All people in this club who are inactive and disinterested members of their community chaperone high school dances.
All young children and teenagers in this club who wish to further their academic careers and educational opportunities are students who attend the school. 
Bonnie is in this club and she either both attends and is very engaged with school events and is a student who attends the school or is not someone who both attends and is very engaged with school events and is not a student who attends the school.
conclusion: Bonnie performs in school talent shows often.
label: Uncertain
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
club(x) ::: x is in this club
talent_show_performer(x) ::: x performs in school talent shows
school_event_attender(x) ::: x attends and is very engaged with school events
inactive_community_member(x) ::: x is an inactive and disinterested community member
dance_chaperone(x) ::: x chaperones high school dances
school_student(x) ::: x is a student who attends the school
young_child_or_teenager(x) ::: x is a young child or teenager
academic_career_seeker(x) ::: x wishes to further their academic careers and educational opportunities

PREMISES:
forall x (club(x) -> (talent_show_performer(x) | inactive_community_member(x)))
forall x (club(x) -> (talent_show_performer(x) | inactive_community_member(x)))
forall x (club(x) & dance_chaperone(x) -> NOT school_student(x))
forall x (club(x) & inactive_community_member(x) -> dance_chaperone(x))
forall x (club(x) & young_child_or_teenager(x) & academic_career_seeker(x) -> school_student(x))
club(bonnie)
bonnie -> (school_event_attender(bonnie) & school_student(bonnie) | NOT (school_event_attender(bonnie) & NOT school_student(bonnie)))

CONCLUSION:
talent_show_performer(bonnie)

context:
People in this club who perform in school talent shows often attend and are very engaged with school events.
People in this club either perform in school talent shows often or are inactive and disinterested community members.
People in this club who chaperone high school dances are not students who attend the school.
All people in this club who are inactive and disinterested members of their community chaperone high school dances.
All young children and teenagers in this club who wish to further their academic careers and educational opportunities are students who attend the school. 
Bonnie is in this club and she either both attends and is very engaged with school events and is a student who attends the school or is not someone who both attends and is very engaged with school events and is not a student who attends the school.
conclusion: If Bonnie is either both a young child or teenager who wishes to further her academic career and educational opportunities and chaperones high school dances or neither is a young child nor teenager who wishes to further her academic career and educational opportunities, then Bonnie is either a student who attends the school or is an inactive and disinterested member of the community.

further_academic_career(x) ::: x wishes to further her academic career and educational opportunities

bonnie -> (school_event_attender(bonnie) AND student(bonnie)) OR (NOT (school_event_attender(bonnie) AND NOT student(bonnie)))

(student(bonnie) OR inactive_community_member(bonnie))

context:
All employees who schedule a meeting with their customers will go to the company building today. 
Everyone who has lunch in the company building schedules meetings with their customers. 
Employees have lunch either in the company building or at home.
If an employee has lunch at home, they are working remotely from home.
All employees who are in other countries work remotely from home. 
No managers work remotely from home. 
James will appear in the company today if and only if he is a manager.
conclusion: James has lunch in the company.
label: Uncertain
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
employee(x) ::: x is an employee
manager(x) ::: x is a manager
has_lunch_in_company(x) ::: x has lunch in the company
has_lunch_at_home(x) ::: x has lunch at home
works_remotely_from_home(x) ::: x works remotely from home
schedules_meetings_with_customers(x) ::: x schedules meetings with their customers
goes_to_company_building(x) ::: x goes to the company building
appears_in_company(x) ::: x appears in the company
in_another_country(x) ::: x is in another country

PREMISES:
forall x (schedules_meetings_with_customers(x) -> goes_to_company_building(x))
forall x (goes_to_company_building(x) -> has_lunch_in_company(x))
forall x (has_lunch_in_company(x) -> schedules_meetings_with_customers(x))
forall x (has_lunch_at_home(x) -> works_remotely_from_home(x))
forall x (in_another_country(x) -> works_remotely_from_home(x))
forall x (works_remotely_from_home(x) -> has_lunch_at_home(x))
forall x (manager(x) -> NOT works_remotely_from_home(x))
forall x (manager(x) -> appears_in_company(x))
appears_in_company(james) -> manager(james)

CONCLUSION:
has_lunch_in_company(james)

romancelanguage(spanish)

context:
In superhero movies, the good guys always win.
The Surprising Adventures of Sir Digby Chicken Caesar is a superhero movie.
Good guys fight bad guys and vice versa.
Sir Digby fights his nemesis.
If a superhero movie is named after a character, that character is a good guy.
The Surprising Adventures of Sir Digby Chicken Caesar is named after Sir Digby.
If somebody wins a fight, the person they are fighting does not win.
If a superhero movie is named after a character, that character is in the movie.
conclusion: Sir Digby’s nemesis does not win.
label: True
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
superhero_movie(x) ::: x is a superhero movie
good_guy(x) ::: x is a good guy
bad_guy(x) ::: x is a bad guy
fights(x, y) ::: x fights y
wins(x, y) ::: x wins y
named_after(x, y) ::: x is named after y
in_movie(x, y) ::: x is in the movie y

PREMISES:
forall x (superhero_movie(x) -> good_guy(x))
superhero_movie(the_surprising_adventures_of_sir_digby_chicken_caesar)
forall x (good_guy(x) -> bad_guy(y) -> fights(x, y))
fights(sir_digby, his_nemesis)
forall x (superhero_movie(x) & named_after(x, y) -> good_guy(y))
named_after(the_surprising_adventures_of_sir_digby_chicken_caesar, sir_digby)
forall x (wins(x, y) -> NOT wins(y, x))
forall x (superhero_movie(x) & named_after(x, y) -> in_movie(y, x))
in_movie(sir_digby, the_surprising_adventures_of_sir_digby_chicken_caesar)

CONCLUSION:
NOT wins(his_nemesis, sir_digby)

smarter(x) ::: x is smarter
knowledge(x) ::: x contains knowledge

PREMISES:
forall x (book(x) -> contains(x, knowledge))
forall x (person(x) & reads(x, y) -> gains_knowledge(x))
forall x (gains_knowledge(x) -> smarter(x))

person(harry)
reads(harry, walden)

CONCLUSION:
contains(walden, knowledge)

reads(x, y) ::: x reads y
gains_knowledge(x) ::: x gains knowledge
smarter(x, y) ::: x is smarter than y
knows(x, y) ::: x knows y

PREMISES:
forall x (book(x) -> contains(x, knowledge))
forall x (reads(x, y) -> gains_knowledge(x))
forall x (gains_knowledge(x) -> smarter(x, x))
book(walden) 
person(harry)
reads(harry, walden)
NOT smarter(harry, harry)

CONCLUSION:
smarter(harry, harry)

context:
A natural language processing task can be categorized as either a language generation task or a language understanding task.
If the output of a natural language processing task is a text sequence, then it is a language generation task.
Machine translation is a natural language processing task.
The output of machine translation is a text sequence.
conclusion: Machine translation is a language generation task.

language_understanding_task(x) ::: x is a language understanding task
natural_language_processing_task(x) ::: x is a natural language processing task
text_sequence(x) ::: x is a text sequence

PREMISES:
forall x (text_sequence(x) -> language_generation_task(x))
natural_language_processing_task(machine_translation)
text_sequence(output_of(machine_translation))

CONCLUSION:
language_generation_task(machine_translation)

context:
Pets are allowed in some managed buildings.
A deposit is required to rent an apartment in a managed building.
The security deposit can be either equal to the monthly rent at a managed building or more.
Fluffy is Tom's cat.
Cats are pets.
The Olive Garden is a managed building.
The monthly rent at the Olive Garden is $2000.
$2000 is more than $1500.
Tom will rent an apartment in a managed building if he is allowed to move in with Fluffy, and the security deposit is no more than $1500.
If a managed building allows pets, then people are allowed to move in with a pet.
conclusion: Tom will rent an apartment in The Olive Garden.
label: False

managed_building(x) ::: x is a managed building
deposit(x) ::: x is a deposit
rent(x) ::: x is a rent
cat(x) ::: x is a cat
apartment(x) ::: x is an apartment
allowed(x,y) ::: x is allowed to move in with y
security_deposit(x) ::: x is a security deposit
more_than(x,y) ::: x is more than y
less_than(x,y) ::: x is less than y

PREMISES:
forall x (pet(x) -> managed_building(y) -> allowed(x,y))
forall x (managed_building(x) -> deposit(x))
forall x (managed_building(x) -> security_deposit(x) = rent(x) | security_deposit(x) > rent(x))
cat(fluffy)
pet(fluffy)
managed_building(the_olive_garden)
rent(the_olive_garden) = 2000
more_than(2000,1500)
forall x (managed_building(x) -> pet(y) -> allowed(x,y))
managed_building(the_olive_garden) -> pet(fluffy) -> allowed(tom,fluffy)
less_than(1500,2000)

CONCLUSION:
allowed(tom,the_olive_garden)

context:
It costs $205 to take the GRE test, which is cheaper than $300.
ETS provides financial aid to those GRE applicants who prove economic hardship.
Those living in single-parent families or having few resources available to them can prove economic hardship.
Tom lives in a single-parent family.
Tom's dad has been out of work, and Tom has few resources available to them. 
Tom is applying to take the GRE test.
conclusion: ETS provides financial aid to Tom.
label: True
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
costs(x, y) ::: x costs y
cheaper(x, y) ::: x is cheaper than y
gre_test(x) ::: x is the GRE test
financial_aid(x, y) ::: x provides financial aid to y
economic_hardship(x) ::: x has economic hardship
single_parent_family(x) ::: x lives in a single-parent family
few_resources(x) ::: x has few resources available
dad_out_of_work(x) ::: x's dad has been out of work
gre_applicant(x) ::: x is applying to take the GRE test

PREMISES:
costs(gre_test, 205)
cheaper(gre_test, 300)
financial_aid(ets, x) <- economic_hardship(x)
economic_hardship(x) <- (single_parent_family(x) OR few_resources(x))
single_parent_family(tom)
dad_out_of_work(tom)
few_resources(tom)
gre_applicant(tom)

CONCLUSION:
financial_aid(ets, tom)

context:
It costs $205 to take the GRE test, which is cheaper than $300.
ETS provides financial aid to those GRE applicants who prove economic hardship.
Those living in single-parent families or having few resources available to them can prove economic hardship.
Tom lives in a single-parent family.
Tom's dad has been out of work, and Tom has few resources available to them. 
Tom is applying to take the GRE test.
conclusion: No one taking the GRE test has financial aid provided to them by something.
label: False
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
costs(x, y) ::: x costs y
cheaper(x, y) ::: x is cheaper than y
gre_test(x) ::: x is the GRE test
financial_aid(x, y) ::: x provides financial aid to y
economic_hardship(x) ::: x has economic hardship
single_parent_family(x) ::: x lives in a single-parent family
few_resources(x) ::: x has few resources
applicant(x) ::: x is applying to take the GRE test
dad_out_of_work(x) ::: x's dad has been out of work

PREMISES:

cheaper(gre_test, 300)
financial_aid(ets, x) <- economic_hardship(x)
economic_hardship(x) <- (single_parent_family(x) OR few_resources(x))
single_parent_family(tom)
dad_out_of_work(tom)
few_resources(tom)
applicant(tom)

CONCLUSION:
NOT financial_aid(ets, tom)

context:
If people spill a lot of food on their clothing, then they are not notably tidy.
All clumsy foodies who go out frequently to find new food restaurants to try are people who spill a lot of food on their clothing.
If people are cleanly, then they are notably tidy.
If people value order and spotlessness, then they are cleanly.
All people whose families prioritize order and spotlessness themselves value order and spotlessness.
Peter is either a person who spills a lot of food on his clothing and is cleanly, or a person who does not spill a lot of food on his clothing and is not cleanly
conclusion: Peter is notably tidy.
label: Uncertain
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
spills_food(x) ::: x spills a lot of food on their clothing
tidy(x) ::: x is notably tidy
person(x) ::: x is a person
foody(x) ::: x is a clumsy foodie who goes out frequently to find new food restaurants to try
cleanly(x) ::: x is cleanly
values_order(x) ::: x values order and spotlessness

PREMISES:
forall x (spills_food(x) -> NOT tidy(x))
forall x (foody(x) -> spills_food(x))
forall x (cleanly(x) -> tidy(x))
forall x (values_order(x) -> cleanly(x))
forall x (forall y (values_order(y)) -> values_order(x))
foody(peter) OR (spills_food(peter) AND cleanly(peter))

CONCLUSION:
tidy(peter)

forall x (foody(x) -> spills_food(x))

forall x (forall y (values_order(y)) -> values_order(x))

If people value order and spotlessness, then they are cleanly.
All people whose families prioritize order and spotlessness themselves value order and spotlessness.

NOT spills_food(peter) -> NOT cleanly(peter)
person(peter)

foody(peter) | family_orders(peter)

context:

All clumsy foodies who go out frequently to find new food restaurants to try are people who spill a lot of food on their clothing.
If people are cleanly, then they are notably tidy.
If people value order and spotlessness, then they are cleanly.
All people whose families prioritize order and spotlessness themselves value order and spotlessness.
Peter is either a person who spills a lot of food on his clothing and is cleanly, or a person who does not spill a lot of food on his clothing and is not cleanly
conclusion: Peter is either both a clumsy foodie who goes out frequently to find new food restaurants to try and issomeone whose family prioritizes order and spotlessness, or he neither is a clumsy foodie who goes out frequently to find new food restaurants to try nor does his family prioritize order and spotlessness.
label: True

family_orders(x) ::: x's family prioritizes order and spotlessness

forall x (foody(x) & person(x) -> spills_food(x))
forall x (person(x) & cleanly(x) -> tidy(x))
forall x (person(x) & values_order(x) -> cleanly(x))
forall x (person(x) & family_orders(x) -> values_order(x))
spills_food(peter) & cleanly(peter) | NOT spills_food(peter) & NOT cleanly(peter)

CONCLUSION:
foody(peter) & family_orders(peter) | NOT (foody(peter) & family_orders(peter))

context:
All fir trees are evergreens.
Some objects of worship are fir trees.
conclusion: Some evergreens are not objects of worship.
label: Uncertain
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
fir_tree(x) ::: x is a fir tree
evergreen(x) ::: x is an evergreen
object_of_worship(x) ::: x is an object of worship

PREMISES:
forall x (fir_tree(x) -> evergreen(x))
exists x (object_of_worship(x) & fir_tree(x))
NOT exists x (evergreen(x) & NOT object_of_worship(x))

CONCLUSION:
exists x (evergreen(x) & NOT object_of_worship(x))

If a man is taller than another man, the taller one can block the other's shooting.
Michael is a man who is taller than everyone else in his class.
If person x is taller than person y, and person y is taller than person z, then x is taller than z
Peter is a man who is taller than Michael.
Michael can block any shooting from a person as long as the person does not jump when shooting.
Michael cannot block Windy's shooting.
Every shooter who can jump when shooting is a great shooter.

jump(x) ::: x can jump when shooting
great_shooter(x) ::: x is a great shooter

taller(peter,michael)

man(windy)

NOT jump(windy)

If something can transport multiple passengers, then they are not one-seaters.
All Tesla Model 3 can transport multiple passengers.
All single-seat electric cars are one-seaters.

single_seat_electric_car(x) ::: x is a single-seat electric car
solo_ev(x) ::: x is a Solo EV car
johns_car(x) ::: x is John's car

forall x (tesla_model_3(x) -> can_transport_multiple_passengers(x))

forall x (solo_ev(x) -> single_seat_electric_car(x))
forall x (not tesla_model_3(x) -> not (tesla_model_3(x) or single_seat_electric_car(x)))

CONCLUSION:

context:
Machine Learning algorithms can be categorized as supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.
Unsupervised learning algorithms do not require labeled data.
The state-of-the-art text summarization model is trained with machine learning algorithms.
Reinforcement learning is not used to train the state-of-the-art text summarization model.
The Machine Learning algorithm for training text summarization models requires labeled data.
conclusion: Supervised learning is used to train the state-of-the-art text summarization model.
label: True
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
machine_learning_algorithm(x) ::: x is a machine learning algorithm
supervised_learning(x) ::: x is a supervised learning algorithm
unsupervised_learning(x) ::: x is an unsupervised learning algorithm
reinforcement_learning(x) ::: x is a reinforcement learning algorithm
text_summarization_model(x) ::: x is a text summarization model
labeled_data(x) ::: x is labeled data
unlabeled_data(x) ::: x is unlabeled data

PREMISES:
forall x (unsupervised_learning(x) -> ~labeled_data(x))
text_summarization_model(x) ::: x is a text summarization model
~reinforcement_learning(text_summarization_model(x))
machine_learning_algorithm(x) ::: x is a machine learning algorithm
labeled_data(x) ::: x is labeled data

CONCLUSION:
supervised_learning(text_summarization_model(x))

unlabeled_data(x) ::: x is unlabeled data

If a film is rated General Audience, then it is appropriate for people of all ages.
All family-friendly animated films are rated General Audience.
All movies in the Frozen series are family-friendly animated films.
“Hachi: A dog’s Tale” is a film.
“Hachi: A dog’s Tale”  is either a family-friendly animated film or is appropriate for people of all ages.

context:
All people who eat salads regularly are very conscious about their health and eating habits.
All people who grew up in health-conscious childhood homes eat salads regularly.
All people who fulfill their daily nutritional intakes grew up in health-conscious childhood homes.
All people who disregard their physical well-being are not very conscious about their health and eating habits.
If people visit the gym at least once a day, then they always fulfill their daily nutritional intakes.
Taylor either grew up in a health-conscious childhood home and disregard her physical well-being, or she did neither.
conclusion: Taylor eats salads regularly.
label: Uncertain
formalisations:
PREDICATES:
eat_salads_regularly(x) ::: x eats salads regularly
is_very_conscious(x) ::: x is very conscious about their health and eating habits
grew_up_health_conscious(x) ::: x grew up in health-conscious childhood homes
fulfills_daily_nutritional_intakes(x) ::: x fulfills their daily nutritional intakes
disregard_physical_wellbeing(x) ::: x disregard their physical well-being
visits_gym_daily(x) ::: x visits the gym at least once a day

PREMISES:
forall x (eat_salads_regularly(x) -> is_very_conscious(x))
forall x (grew_up_health_conscious(x) -> eat_salads_regularly(x))
forall x (fulfills_daily_nutritional_intakes(x) -> grew_up_health_conscious(x))
forall x (disregard_physical_wellbeing(x) -> NOT is_very_conscious(x))
forall x (visits_gym_daily(x) -> fulfills_daily_nutritional_intakes(x))
grew_up_health_conscious(taylor) OR NOT disregard_physical_wellbeing(taylor)

context:
A podcast is not a novel.
If a person is born in American City, the person is American.
If a book is a novel and it is written by a person, then the person is a novel writer.
Dani Shapiro is an American writer.
Family History is written by Dani Shapiro.
Family History is a novel written in 2003.
Dani Shapiro created a podcast called Family Secrets.
Boston is an American city.
conclusion: Dani Shapiro is a novel writer.
label: True

podcast(x) ::: x is a podcast
novel(x) ::: x is a novel
american(x) ::: x is American
person(x) ::: x is a person
writer(x) ::: x is a writer
novel_writer(x) ::: x is a novel writer
book(x) ::: x is a book
city(x) ::: x is a city
year(x) ::: x is a year

PREMISES:
forall x (podcast(x) -> NOT novel(x))
forall x (american_city(x) -> american(x))
forall x (novel(x) & person(y) & written_by(x, y) -> novel_writer(y))
american_writer(dani_shapiro)
written_by(family_history, dani_shapiro)
novel(family_history) & book(family_history) & year(family_history, 2003)
written_by(family_secrets, dani_shapiro)
american_city(boston)

CONCLUSION:
novel_writer(dani_shapiro)
